reasoning: The EFFR dropped to 3.87% around late October/early November 2025, reflecting a Fed rate cut. The US central bank is clearly in an active rate-cutting cycle. Over the near term (Nov 16, Dec 9), no major FOMC meetings are scheduled to introduce further changes, so the rate is likely to hover around 3.87% (with only a small, random 1-2 basis point risk of shifting up slightly). By February 2026, additional rate cuts are broadly expected, which would lower the rate to further depths (e.g., 3.62% or lower). While typical macro trends make a rate increase highly unlikely, structural uncertainty regarding sudden policy pivots or inflation spikes necessitates that our probabilities slowly regress toward 0.5 at longer horizons.
